data.title: CBOT wheat edges lower as traders eye Chinese demand
data.description:
Chicago Board of Trade wheat futures closed lower on Wednesday as market players assessed Chinese demand for American products amid an ongoing trade dispute between the two countries.

  • CBOT March soft red winter wheat (WH25) settled down 4-3/4 cents to $5.72-1/4 a bushel.

  • K.C. March hard red winter wheat (KWH25) ended down 3 cents at $5.91-3/4 per bushel.

  • Minneapolis March spring wheat (MWEH25) ended unchanged at $6.21-3/4 a bushel.

  • In a sign of weak Chinese demand, trade sources told Reuters that China had delayed imports of up to 600,000 metric tons of mostly Australian wheat and offered some of these cargoes to other buyers.

  • Agricultural markets had feared that tariffs proposed by U.S. President Donald Trump against Canada, Mexico and China could hurt demand for U.S. farm goods in a tit-for-tat trade battle.

  • Trump on Monday postponed tariffs against Mexico and Canada for a month, while Beijing announced limited retaliatory tariffs on U.S. goods on Tuesday that did not include crops.

  • A softer dollar tends to make U.S. grains more competitive globally, and commodity funds hold a sizable net short position in CBOT wheat futures.

data.title: U.S. Housing Market Likely to Keep Inflation Steady — Market Talk
data.description: Investors grew worried about inflation during the trade war flare-up, but the U.S. housing market is likely to counter price pressures elsewhere, Angel Oak's Clayton Triick says. He expects new-home supply to increase, cooling prices and helping keep the Fed on track to cut rates. Housing market dynamics also make investing in mortgages an attractive option for fixed-income investors. Triick is particularly bullish on higher-yielding mortgages not backed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. "You are getting elevated yields and the fundamentals of housing are still really strong," he says. A drop on house prices could increase defaults, but Triick says it is unlikely to happen. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Mondelez International, Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Mondelez International, Inc., a global leader in the snack food industry, has released its 2024 Form 10-K report, detailing the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces. The report provides a comprehensive overview of Mondelez's efforts to drive growth, enhance operational efficiency, and navigate a complex global market environment.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Revenues: $36,441 million, increased by 1.2% from 2023 driven by higher net pricing and the acquisition of Evirth, partially offset by unfavorable currency-related items.
  • Operating Income: $6,345 million, increased by 15.3% from 2023 due to higher net pricing and lower manufacturing costs driven by productivity.
  • Net Earnings Attributable to Mondelēz International: $4,611 million, decreased by 7.0% from 2023, impacted by higher intangible asset impairment charges and costs incurred for the ERP Systems Implementation program.
  • Diluted EPS: $3.42, decreased by 5.5% from 2023, affected by lapping prior-year gains and higher costs related to strategic initiatives.
  • Adjusted EPS: $3.36, increased by 9.1% from 2023, driven by operating gains, fewer shares outstanding, and lower taxes.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: Mondelēz International's operations are organized into four segments: Latin America, AMEA (Asia Pacific, Middle East, and Africa), Europe, and North America. The company focuses on core product categories including biscuits & baked snacks, chocolate, gum & candy, beverages, and cheese & grocery.
  • Geographical Performance: In 2024, 74% of Mondelēz's net revenues were generated outside the United States, with significant contributions from emerging markets such as Latin America, parts of Europe, and the AMEA region. The company experienced sales impacts in certain AMEA markets due to geopolitical conflicts but maintained overall growth.
  • New Product Launches: Mondelēz introduced new varieties of core products, including zero-sugar Oreos in China and reduced sugar candies in Australia. The company also expanded its portfolio with new formats like Milka brownies and Oreo cakes.
  • New Production Launches: The company completed the acquisition of Evirth, a leading manufacturer of cakes and pastries in China, enhancing its production capabilities in the region.
  • Future Outlook: Mondelēz aims to drive long-term growth by focusing on consumer-centric growth, operational excellence, a winning growth culture, and sustainable snacking. The company plans to continue optimizing its portfolio and expanding its presence in high-growth channels and under-represented segments.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Systems Transformation Program: The company has approved a $1.2 billion multi-year systems transformation program to upgrade its global ERP and supply chain systems, expected to be completed by year-end 2028. This initiative is part of the company's strategy to drive operational excellence and scale sustainable snacking.
  • Capital Management: The company paid dividends of $2,349 million in 2024, reflecting an 11% increase in the quarterly cash dividend to $0.470 per share. It also repurchased shares under its authorized buyback program. The company maintained a debt-to-capitalization ratio of 0.40 at the end of 2024, with total debt reduced to $17.7 billion from $19.4 billion in 2023. The Board of Directors approved a new $2 billion long-term financing authorization, replacing the prior authorization.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue utilizing its commercial paper program and international credit lines to meet short-term financing needs. It expects capital expenditures to be up to $1.4 billion in 2025, including investments in the ERP System Implementation program. The company aims to maintain sufficient liquidity for its working capital needs, planned capital expenditures, and shareholder returns, while monitoring global economic conditions and geopolitical developments.

Challenges and Risks

  • Competitive Industry: Mondelēz International operates in a highly competitive industry, facing risks related to the execution of its strategy and the ability to respond to channel shifts, pricing, and other competitive pressures. The company must adapt to changing market conditions, including technological advancements and evolving consumer preferences, to maintain its market position.
  • Commodity Price Volatility: The company faces significant strategic and operational risks due to volatile commodity prices and potential constraints in the availability of key commodities like cocoa, dairy, and wheat. These risks are exacerbated by geopolitical conditions, inflationary pressures, and climate change.
  • Geopolitical Risks: The ongoing war in Ukraine and geopolitical tensions, such as those between China and Taiwan, present further risks, including supply chain disruptions and increased input costs. The company also faces challenges from increased focus on sustainability and climate change, which could affect its reputation and ability to procure materials.
  • Currency Exchange Rate Fluctuations: Mondelēz International is exposed to currency exchange rate fluctuations due to its global operations. The company hedges against these risks but acknowledges that its strategies may not fully protect against economic losses.
  • Tax Law Changes: Changes in tax laws and rates, as well as disagreements with taxing authorities, could materially affect its financial results.

SEC Filing: Mondelez International, Inc. [ MDLZ ] - 10-K - Feb. 05, 2025

data.title: Fed's Barkin sees potential soft spot in the U.S. economy
data.description:
By Greg Robb

Despite reports of optimism, businesses have pulled back on investing

One area of concern for the U.S. economy is that big businesses may be pulling back from making new investments given the uncertainty surrounding the economic policies of the Trump administration, Richmond Fed President Tom Barkin said on Wednesday.

A new survey of chief financial officers carried out by the Richmond and Atlanta regional Fed banks and Duke University found "total optimism" on the economy and the new administration, most likely because of an expected business-friendly atmosphere, Barkin said.

But when asked about the outlook for their own companies, optimism "pretty much flatlined," Barkin said.

This translates into an overall message of "we think this will be good, but I don't know how it's going to play out in my business," Barkin said during an interview on Bloomberg Television.

Barkin is a former top executive at management-consulting company McKinsey & Co., and his comments about business sentiment are closely followed by economists.

Following the presidential election, a separate survey of business sentiment from the National Federation of Independent Business reported the biggest jump in its 40-year history.

Small businesses do more of the hiring in the economy, while big businesses do more of the investments, Barkin noted.

"I think it's possible that we get another year like 2019, where consumers are spending and people are hiring, but the investment side of it is a question mark," he said.

Business investment lost considerable momentum in the second half of 2024, falling at a 0.8% annual rate in the fourth quarter, Brian Bethune, an economist at Boston College, said in an email last week following a report on gross domestic product. In the fourth quarter of 2019, shortly before the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic, business investment fell at a 2.7% annual rate.

In 2024, the likely culprit for weak investment activity was the persistence of relatively high real interest rates, Bethune said.

Another factor was the spike in uncertainty about the policy environment this year, he said, which may have led to the postponement of major investment outlays.

Barkin also said that uncertainty was an issue.

"I'm hearing from everyone I talk to about just elevated policy uncertainty," he said.

In addition to President Donald Trump's proposed tariffs, details of deregulation plans and tax policies remain unclear.

"There's just a lot of uncertainty in the air, and it's very hard to know what's happening with growth and employment, what's happening with inflation, until you get a little more clarity on all these uncertainties," Barkin said.

In the Bloomberg interview, Barkin said the only reason the Federal Reserve might hike interest rates in the near term would be if the economy was overheating. At the moment, there are no signs of that in the data, he added.

"I see inflation coming down, not going up. I see the job market stabilizing. It doesn't feel like we're overheating," Barkin said.

Barkin stressed, however, that he wasn't taking anything off the table.

He said the Fed's most recent forecast, in December, included a "lean" toward more cuts. But he said December "seems like a lifetime ago."

Barkin was asked if he had a bias toward monetary-policy easing.

"My bias is to see what happens and react appropriately," he said.

BX:TMUBMUSD02Y SPX

-Greg Robb

data.title: Bank-Regulation Reforms Could Promote Treasury Liquidity — Market Talk
data.description:
1500 ET - Fed governor Michelle Bowman notes that Treasury-market liquidity has been a steady source of concern for years, and suggests that regulatory reforms for banks could ease the problem. Speaking at a banking conference in Topeka, Kan., Bowman notes that when bank balance sheets expand, regulatory leverage ratios can become a binding constraint, even if the bank is investing in safe assets. "When constrained in this way, bank-affiliated primary dealers may pull back on market intermediation activities," Bowman says. "Where we can take proactive regulatory measures to ensure that primary dealers have adequate balance sheet capacity to intermediate Treasury markets, we should do so." She suggests amending the leverage ratio and G-SIB surcharge regulations for big U.S. banks could help. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1458 ET - Investors grew worried about inflation during the trade war flare-up, but the U.S. housing market is likely to counter price pressures elsewhere, Angel Oak's Clayton Triick says. He expects new-home supply to increase, cooling prices and helping keep the Fed on track to cut rates. Housing market dynamics also make investing in mortgages an attractive option for fixed-income investors. Triick is particularly bullish on higher-yielding mortgages not backed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. "You are getting elevated yields and the fundamentals of housing are still really strong," he says. A drop on house prices could increase defaults, but Triick says it is unlikely to happen. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1454 ET - First-term Trump tariffs didn't matter much for aggregate inflation, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee says, speaking at a conference in Detroit. But he warns the impact could be worse this time around. As companies adapted their supply chains, they likely shifted "all the easiest things out of China," Goolsbee says, leaving behind production inputs that are harder to move. Such trade will remain vulnerable to tariffs because there are no ready substitutes, Goolsbee says: "Tariffs on intermediate goods raise the costs of production for domestic manufacturers of other goods, so the inflation impact is unlikely to stay only in the directly affected products." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1451 ET - Bond prices look "pretty attractive" this year in the U.S., Angel Oak's Clayton Triick says. He expects the Fed to trim interest rates by 25 or 50 basis points in 2025, as inflation remains sticky but is unlikely to accelerate, he says. Triick argues that yields are elevated and likely to decline in coming months, in which case bond prices will be going up. "It is probably going to be a really good year for bonds," he says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1447 ET - Tariffs would likely complicate the Fed's job in responding to further stubbornness in inflation, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee says. Speaking at a conference in Detroit, Goolsbee says that depending on White House policy, the Fed will have to determine whether tariff-induced price increases are a one-time adjustment or reflect a deeper pattern before reacting. "If we see inflation rising or progress stalling in 2025, the Fed will be in the difficult position of trying to figure out if the inflation is coming from overheating or if it's coming from tariffs," Goolsbee says. "That distinction will be critical for deciding when or even if the Fed should act." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1333 ET - Global sustainable bond issuance is expected to remain steady in 2025, at about $1 trillion, according to S&P Global Ratings in a report. The agency says "with green bonds and supranational entities in the lead, the sustainable bond market seems to have returned to its roots." Corporate green bond issuance is now well established, S&P says. However, issuance fell in 2024 to about 11% of the global total, from 13% in 2023. More issuance in low-income and emerging economies is expected to address the climate finance gap, S&P says, but adds "we see a risk that macroeconomic and political factors could stifle further growth." (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1216 ET - Mexican household consumption rose 0.5% in November after two successive monthly declines, statistics institute Inegi reports. Fixed investment edged up 0.1% but was down 0.3% from a year earlier. "Headwinds for domestic demand persist, with consumption more resilient, but investment facing greater challenges," analysts at Banorte say in a note, referring to trade risks and a tight government budget. "Although tariffs against our country are on hold, we believe that the business climate will remain deteriorated until a definitive resolution is reached," and that probably won't happen until the USMCA review, they add. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1213 ET - Look for another month of strong employment growth on Friday, but don't be surprised if the labor market loses some momentum in the months ahead, Piper Sandler economists write. Their model projects that the economy added 209,000 jobs in January, more than the 169,000 consensus forecast in WSJ's poll. But Piper Sandler notes that small business hiring looked tepid in the January ADP data, "a concerning signal for overall employment," the economists say. Meanwhile, in the weekly UI benefits data, continuing unemployment claims have been elevated, which suggests more modest payroll numbers in the months ahead, Piper Sandler notes. ( matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1040 ET - The National Bank of Poland's more restrictive policy stance compared to other central banks has boosted the Polish zloty, ING analyst Frantisek Taborsky says. The NBP on Wednesday left its benchmark interest rate at 5.75%, as widely expected, having held rates steady throughout 2024. With the market pricing in more rate cuts for the European Central Bank, the zloty is supported by favorable rate differentials, Taborsky says. Another supportive factor is Poland's economic outperformance versus Central and Eastern European peers. "As a wild card for the zloty is the possible progress of a Ukraine-Russia deal, which would be positive for CEE especially Poland." EUR/PLN falls 0.2% to 4.2023, having earlier hit a 2018 low of 4.1931, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1038 ET - The U.S. Treasury refunding announcement "was mostly uneventful," Santander's Stephen Stanley writes. The U.S. Treasury says it is offering $125 billion in securities to refund around $106.2 billion of notes and bonds maturing this month, while raising around $18.8 billion in new cash. The Treasury warns of greater-than-normal issuance variability until the debt limit is suspended or increased. Stanley says the Treasury's current schedule "should be fine for most or all of calendar 2025." He says coupon auction sizes will likely need to increase. Treasury yields are falling, showing no reaction to the refunding announcement. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1032 ET - Tariff "front-running" likely contributed to December's increase in Canadian trade activity, which alongside the weaker Canadian dollar supported economic growth into end-2024, BMO Economics' Shelly Kaushik says. Canada swung to a C$708 million goods-trade surplus with the world in December, though the full-year shortfall deepened to C$7.2 billion. Kaushik says not to be surprised if early 2025 trade figures are subject to heightened volatility as everyone awaits more certainty on the U.S. tariff front. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbNMStewart)

1028 ET - U.S. growth came in surprisingly strong in 2024 versus start-of-year projections, and most economists expect the momentum to carry into 2025. But while the consumer economy and the services sector are thriving, industrial companies are complaining of a grimmer backdrop. "Recent economic activity is primarily being led by the strength in service-related sectors," while the physical economy has been weaker, Dow CFO Jeff Tate tells analysts on a recent earnings call. Dow executives several times on the call referred to a "prolonged economic downturn." Eastman Chemical CEO Mark Costa gave a similar take on the company's recent analyst call. "The macro economy is certainly challenging right now as we all know," he said. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

data.title: Controversial OPM email server operates 'entirely' on government computers, agency says
data.description:
Key points:
  • OPM says email system uses only government computers
  • System lacks required Privacy Impact Assessment, complaint argues
  • Concerns over Musk's handling of personal data and security protocols

The controversial email system used by Elon Musk and his associates to simultaneously contact all federal employees for the first time operates "entirely on government computers" and contrary to news reports does not use a non-government server, officials with the Office of Personnel Management said in a court filing Wednesday.

An attorney representing unnamed OPM employees asked a judge January 27 to issue a temporary restraining order halting the system as it represented a grave security risk to roughly 2 million executive branch and judicial branch employees, along with an unknown number of contractors.

The email system was developed and installed without the required Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA), Kelly McClanahan, the executive director of the National Security Counselors public interest law firm, argued in a complaint.

The PIA was not necessary since the system only deals with federal employee data, government lawyers said in a February 5 court filing, which included a PIA dated February 5 which said the system, dubbed the "Government-Wide Email System (GWES)," collects federal employee names, their government email addresses, and "short, voluntary email responses.

The filing comes amid growing concern that Musk and his associates are flouting security protocols when handling personal information and data as they work to cut staff across the U.S. government.

The PIA listed Senior Advisor to the Director Riccardo Biasini as the system's contact point and was signed by Greg Hogan, the agency's chief information officer. Biasini is a former engineer at Tesla and most recently a director at The Boring Company, Musk's tunnel-building operation in Las Vegas.

It's not clear if the "government computers" are new or were existing OPM infrastructure. An OPM spokesperson did not immediately respond to questions.

McClanahan argued the system contains "vast quantities" of personally identifiable information "which are more susceptible to cyberattacks than the pre-existing OPM systems.”

The suit cited an anonymous Reddit post saying that someone "walked into our building and plugged in an email server to our network" to send the emails. An anonymous OPM staffer told the Musk Watch newsletter Feb. 3 that the server was "a piece of commercial hardware they believed was not obtained through the proper federal procurement process."

McClanahan did not immediately respond to a request for comment Wednesday.

data.title: BOLLE: A Fresh Perspective on No-Alcohol Sparkling Blanc De Blancs and Rose Wine
data.description:
NAPA, CALIFORNIA / ACCESS Newswire / February 5, 2025 / BOLLE Drinks is thrilled to launch its innovative, award winning non-alcoholic (<0.5% ABV) sparkling wines in the United States. With bottles first arriving in the late fall 2024, BOLLE is poised to redefine the no-alcohol wine category with a fresh technique, the world's first and only non-alcoholic wine to introduce secondary fermentation. Departing from conventional norms, BOLLE has multiple patents filed and pending for its winemaking techniques as it aspires to challenge perceptions and offer a delicious real wine taste experience for enthusiasts wanting a non-alcoholic wine that doesn't compromise on taste or quality.

Bolle Sparkling Rose

Bolle Sparkling Rose

BOLLE Drinks is located in London, England, the grapes are harvested in La Mancha, Spain, and the wine is crafted in the Rheinhessen region in Germany. BOLLE released two cuvées to the UK and the US last year: a Sparkling Rosé and a Blanc de Blancs. The team consists of Founder, Roberto Vanin who developed the winemaking process, Gary Read as CEO and Steve Burch as Head of US Operations. This collaboration brings together a group of seasoned professionals in winemaking, business and innovation. Roberto Vanin's expertise in oenology, chemistry and R&D, complemented by CEO Gary Read's go-to-market and company building acumen, and Head of US Operations Steve Burch's extensive background as a California winemaker.

data.title: Bonds Expected to Have a Good Year — Market Talk
data.description:
1451 ET - Bond prices look "pretty attractive" this year in the U.S., Angel Oak's Clayton Triick says. He expects the Fed to trim interest rates by 25 or 50 basis points in 2025, as inflation remains sticky but is unlikely to accelerate, he says. Triick argues that yields are elevated and likely to decline in coming months, in which case bond prices will be going up. "It is probably going to be a really good year for bonds," he says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1447 ET - Tariffs would likely complicate the Fed's job in responding to further stubbornness in inflation, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee says. Speaking at a conference in Detroit, Goolsbee says that depending on White House policy, the Fed will have to determine whether tariff-induced price increases are a one-time adjustment or reflect a deeper pattern before reacting. "If we see inflation rising or progress stalling in 2025, the Fed will be in the difficult position of trying to figure out if the inflation is coming from overheating or if it's coming from tariffs," Goolsbee says. "That distinction will be critical for deciding when or even if the Fed should act." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1333 ET - Global sustainable bond issuance is expected to remain steady in 2025, at about $1 trillion, according to S&P Global Ratings in a report. The agency says "with green bonds and supranational entities in the lead, the sustainable bond market seems to have returned to its roots." Corporate green bond issuance is now well established, S&P says. However, issuance fell in 2024 to about 11% of the global total, from 13% in 2023. More issuance in low-income and emerging economies is expected to address the climate finance gap, S&P says, but adds "we see a risk that macroeconomic and political factors could stifle further growth." (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1332 ET - Stanley Black & Decker is making $150 million in targeted investments this year aimed at accelerating its supply chain overhaul to reduce the company's reliance on production in China, CFO Patrick Hallinan says on an analyst call. The tool maker anticipates that Donald Trump's targeted tariffs on China could cost the company $90 million to $100 million in 2025 if they remain in place, though Stanley believes it could get the impact down to $10 million to $20 million with higher prices and further changes to its supply chain, the CFO says. Stanley is waiting for greater clarity on tariffs before enacting any new measures to battle the cost inflation, the CFO says. "We expect the current situation to remain dynamic," he says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1309 ET - Chipotle Mexican Grill outlines the impact tariffs might have on its business, depending how things play out. CFO Adam Rymer on an earnings call says if tariffs go into full effect, it would have an ongoing impact of about 60 basis points on the burrito chain's cost of sales. He notes Chipotle sourced about 2% of its sales from Mexico, which includes avocados, tomatoes, limes and peppers, while less than 0.5% of sales are from Canada and China. Rymer also says Chipotle is confident it can offset the potential headwind through investments made in 2024 including through efficiencies identified in its supply chain. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1306 ET - Chipotle is off 2%, and may continue to feel pressure near-term despite an in-line 4Q, BTIG's Peter Saleh says in a research note. The analyst believes 1Q estimates will have to be adjusted due to freezing temperatures, California wildfires and a calendar shift. He says the company will have one less day in February due to Leap Day last year. "Given the current trends and comparisons in February and March, we see 1Q25 guidance as somewhat aggressive, with little room for error," says the analyst. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1251 ET - Snap's 4Q beat and optimistic outlook were encouraging, say analysts at BofA Securities in a research note, but user growth remains limited in high value US/EU markets. The analysts also say the app transition rollout suggests mixed results, and that big EBITDA upside seems less likely given growing expenses. BofA says Snap's stock-based compensation expense exceeds the investment bank's free cash flow estimates through 2027. Snap slides 7%. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1213 ET - Apple shares are under modest pressure amid reports that Chinese regulators are considering opening a formal investigation into App Store's fees and policies, even if revenue from the App Store in China is a manageable $5 billion, Wedbush analysts say in a research note. "So its less about revenue exposure for investors and more about building US/China tensions with US Big Tech in line for retaliatory shots across the bow," the analysts say, noting that China recently opened an antitrust probe into Alphabet's Google. "This is all a game of high stakes poker between Trump and China and Apple is clearly a chip on the table," the analysts add. Apple shares fall 1% to $230.39. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1213 ET - Look for another month of strong employment growth on Friday, but don't be surprised if the labor market loses some momentum in the months ahead, Piper Sandler economists write. Their model projects that the economy added 209,000 jobs in January, more than the 169,000 consensus forecast in WSJ's poll. But Piper Sandler notes that small business hiring looked tepid in the January ADP data, "a concerning signal for overall employment," the economists say. Meanwhile, in the weekly UI benefits data, continuing unemployment claims have been elevated, which suggests more modest payroll numbers in the months ahead, Piper Sandler notes. ( matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1159 ET - The threat of U.S. tariffs on imports from its northern neighbor brought out waves of calls on social media for Canadians to buy local products in place of American goods. Canada's largest private sector union wants to go further, and has written to the country's largest grocery chains urging them to buy Canadian. Unifor in a letter to the CEOs of Empire, Loblaw and Metro encouraged them to prioritize Canadian products in response to President Trump's threatened levies. It also wants grocers to make it easier for consumers to prioritize Canadian items on their shelves, supporting domestic industries and jobs. "Canadian workers and their families are ready to defend against the Trump tariff threat and we need Canadian grocers to do their part," says Unifor national President Lana Payne. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1156 ET - Stanley Black & Decker had a mixed quarter in which a big tax benefit drove its earnings beat, Edward Jones analyst Faisal Hersi says in a research note. Organic revenue was higher than anticipated, which was a nice surprise, but operating profitability fell short of expectations, even as it improved sequentially due to the company's cost-cutting efforts and improving supply-chain expenses, Hersi says. The tool market is still choppy and do-it-yourself work remains weak, while professional demand is solid, the analyst says. He expects mixed demand across the company's markets to persist near-term, though tools volume should keep recovering this year. Shares fall 2% to $84.97. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1153 ET - The market's stock reaction to PayPal earnings was overblown, especially considering underlying numbers such as transaction margin dollar growth, Bernstein analysts say in a research note. The digital-payments company also continued growing incrementality of many initiatives such as modern experiences, debit cards and Venmo. "These initiatives, along with consumer habituation drivers such as debit cards, can support Branded volume growth in 2025," the analysts say. Overall, PayPal appears to be under more solid footing under the new management team, the analysts add, noting that they expect slightly more favorable risk-reward in the near-term. Shares rise 2.2% following a 13% drop on Tuesday. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

data.title: Allied Properties REIT Brief: National Bank Says "Earnings Outlook Distracts From Positive Ops Update Highlighting Structural Sensitivities"
Amazon set to release long-delayed Alexa generative AI revamp
data.description:
Key points:
  • Amazon prepares to release Alexa generative AI revamp after delays
  • Amazon invites press to event in late February
  • Alexa could come with price tag of $5 to $10 per month

Amazon is set to release its long-awaited - and delayed - Alexa generative artificial intelligence voice service, said three people familiar with the matter, and has scheduled a press event for later this month to preview it.

Once released, it would mark the most significant upgrade to the product since its initial introduction accelerated a wave of digital assistants more than a decade ago.

Amazon on Wednesday sent press invites to an event to be held on February 26 in New York featuring the head of its devices and services team, Panos Panay. A spokesperson said the event is Alexa-focused, while declining to elaborate.

The new generative AI-powered Alexa represents at once a huge opportunity for Amazon, which counts more than half a billion Alexa-enabled devices in the market, and a tremendous risk. Amazon is hoping the revamp, designed to be able to converse with users, can convert some of its hundreds of millions of users into paying customers in an effort to generate a return for the unprofitable business.

The AI service will be able to respond to multiple prompts in sequence and, company executives have said, even act as an “agent” on behalf of users by taking actions for them without their direct involvement. That contrasts with the current iteration, which generally handles only a single request at a time.

Executives have scheduled a meeting, known as a "Go/No-go," for February 14. There they will make a final decision on the "street readiness" of Alexa's generative AI revamp, according to the people and an internal planning document seen by Reuters.

Alexa's revamp carries with it all the challenges inherent in now-familiar generative AI chatbots from OpenAI, Alphabet and others including the possibility of fabricated answers, known as hallucinations. With access to Alexa available in cars, televisions, thermostats and mobile phones, it could become an essential daily tool for scheduling and even shopping.

Initially, Amazon plans to roll out the new Alexa service to a limited number of users and will not charge for it, the people said, though it has considered a $5 to $10 monthly fee. The company will also continue to offer what it is calling “Classic Alexa,” the version broadly available today for free. One of the people said Amazon has discontinued adding new offerings to Classic Alexa.

BEZOS' VISION

While Apple's Siri voice assistant preceded Alexa's 2014 release by three years, the Amazon service supercharged the acceptance of voice assistants. But for many people, Alexa is now used for little more than kitchen timers and weather updates due to its lack of significant overhauls in the last few years.

Alexa is the brainchild of Amazon founder Jeff Bezos, who envisioned a service that would resemble the voice-activated computers on TV's "Star Trek." The hope was that once perfected, users would turn to the voice assistant for hundreds of everyday tasks like turning on lights, preheating the oven, accessing the internet, playing music, writing emails and summoning taxis.

“Someday in the future - that might be years or decades away - it could answer everything that you would ever ask it,” Amazon’s then-chief of devices, Dave Limp, said nearly a decade ago.

With those weighty expectations, the move to upgrade Alexa has suffered delays over concerns around the quality and speed of its responses, people familiar with the matter have told Reuters. Amazon dubbed the new service “Banyan” internally, as well as “Remarkable Alexa,” though it was not immediately clear if the Seattle company planned on using either as a new product name.

In a January Financial Times interview, Amazon executive Rohit Prasad acknowledged some of the obstacles in developing what is effectively an entirely new service, including the work to eliminate hallucinations.

Analysts at Bank of America estimate Amazon could generate $600 million annually if 10% of active users, which it estimates at around 100 million devices, pay $5 per month for the service.

CUSTOMER PREFERENCES

The new Alexa is designed to remember customer preferences to help make music or restaurant recommendations and allow users to give multiple prompts in a single session, so that, say, one could later modify a hamburger order before it is sent out for delivery, the people said.

It will be compatible with existing Alexa devices, said the people.

Amazon will rely, at least in part, on artificial intelligence software from startup Anthropic – in which it has invested $8 billion – to underpin the AI in Alexa, people familiar with the matter told Reuters.

In September 2023, Amazon demonstrated an early version of the souped-up Alexa , but concerns over quality caused Amazon to repeatedly push back its launch date and that rendition was never released to the public.

data.title: Chicago Fed's Goolsbee: Tariff Impact Could be Worse This Time — Market Talk
data.description: First-term Trump tariffs didn't matter much for aggregate inflation, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee says, speaking at a conference in Detroit. But he warns the impact could be worse this time around. As companies adapted their supply chains, they likely shifted "all the easiest things out of China," Goolsbee says, leaving behind production inputs that are harder to move. Such trade will remain vulnerable to tariffs because there are no ready substitutes, Goolsbee says: "Tariffs on intermediate goods raise the costs of production for domestic manufacturers of other goods, so the inflation impact is unlikely to stay only in the directly affected products." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

data.title: Broadcom and 9 More Stocks That Gain From Alphabet's Big Investments — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Jacob Sonenshine

Alphabet shareholders aren't happy about the company's plans for enormous investments this year, but a host of other businesses will directly benefit.

After the close of trading on Tuesday, Alphabet reported mixed financial results and said that its capital expenditures will total $75 billion this year. That is more than $15 billion above what analysts had forecast and is almost 45% higher than last year's capex. Combined capital spending for Alphabet, Meta Platforms, Amazon.com and Microsoft is expected to reach hundreds of billions of dollars this year, for double-digit percentage growth.

For these companies, and certainly Alphabet, this will weigh on free cash flow and earnings. Alphabet's depreciation expense will rise significantly this year, and as far as the market is concerned, the company had better maintain its competitiveness with its artificial intelligence offerings for both its search and cloud businesses, so that it can earn a high rate of return on the investments.

Although the stock was in the red as investors reduced their expectations for this year's earnings, the positive is that management's willingness to invest aggressively indicates that the Chinese app DeepSeek hasn't posed a major threat to demand for Alphabet's AI offerings.

The news is clearly a relief for all of the companies that produce hardware to power AI. The faster capex increases, the higher the growth of demand for semiconductors, data centers, and all the products that go into building them.

Those stocks have taken a hit recently — Nvidia stock is still 19% below the record closing level it hit in January — because the market is concerned that DeepSeek's lower-cost and cheaper AI offering will ultimately force companies such as Alphabet and Microsoft to scale back their investment plans. That would mean less hardware demand than the market is counting on now.

But the capex guidance from Alphabet and other U.S. technology services companies this earnings season proves that growth remains steep for of all things data center.

Eventually, data-center spending will slow down, but for the moment, it is obvious that DeepSeek isn't at a large enough scale to disrupt U.S. companies' investments. That will give them time to fine-tune their AI offerings and make them more competitive.

For now, high growth in spending on data centers lives on. That is why most stocks that stand to gain as companies build more computing capacity were rallying Wednesday. Nvidia was off of its recent low, up 3%.

Chip maker Broadcom, which got more than a fifth of its $53.2 billion of 2024 sales from AI chips, boasted a jump of more than 200% in sales of those semiconductors last year. Continued explosive AI growth will go a long way in pumping profits far higher, so the stock was up just over 6% Wednesday.

Micron Technology stock rose 2% Wednesday, outpacing the iShares Semiconductor exchange-traded fund's just under 2% gain. Micron, which is ramping up sales of high bandwidth memory that attaches to Nvidia's AI chips, still gets only a small chunk of its total revenue from AI. That has limited its gains relative to other AI names.

Arm Holdings stock was up more than 6%. Analysts expect sales to continue to grow for the coming years, as Arm sells CPU products into Nvidia's chips and licenses its chip designs to semiconductor companies and smartphone makers.

Stock in Amphenol, which is ramping up sales of connectors that go into Nvidia chips, rose initially, before dipping into the red. It remained above a one-month low reached when concernS about DeepSeek hammered the stock market last week.

Analysts expect sales of connectors for Nvidia chips will account for just over $1 billion of its $18.3 billion in total sales this year, according to FactSet. The vast majority of Amphenol's business is still tied to areas such as automobiles, so the stock slipped along with shares of hundreds of other U.S. companies.

The key to remember is that Alphabet's capex guidance is a positive for Amphenol's earnings.

Stock in Vertiv Holdings, a manufacturer of cooling equipment that gets the majority of its sales from data centers, was up almost 6%. Shares of Eaton, a rival that is less dependent on data-center sales, rose initially, but then dipped into the red. Stock in Schneider Electric, a third maker of cooling equipment, was also in the red, but above its one-month low.

Corning and nVent, both of which see a slice of revenue from AI, were both up around 2%.

However these stocks trade in the short term, expect them to ride a tailwind of AI-related demand.

Write to Jacob Sonenshine at jacob.sonenshine@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Daily Roundup of Key US Economic Data for Feb. 5
data.description:
ADP private payrolls rose by 183,000 in January after an upwardly revised 176,000 jobs gain in December.

The services sectors added 190,000 jobs, led by gains in leisure and hospitality and trade, transportation and utilities hiring. Goods sector payrolls fell by 6,000 jobs, with construction payrolls up only 3,000 and manufacturing payrolls down 13,000.

The ISM's services index fell to 52.8 in January from 54.0 in December, with declines in the readings for production, new orders and prices but a gain for employment. All remained above the breakeven point.

The S&P Global nonmanufacturing index was revised higher in January to 52.9 from the flash 52.8 estimate, as expected, but was still below the 56.8 reading in December.

The international trade deficit widened to $98.43 billion in December from $78.94 billion in November, larger than an expected $96.8 billion gap as imports rose and exports declined.

Imports of industrial supplies, consumer goods and capital goods all increased in December, while imports of auto supplies and food and beverages decreased. Exports of consumer goods, industrial supplies, capital goods, auto supplies and food and beverages all decreased.

The Mortgage Bankers Association reported a 2.2% increase in mortgage applications in the week ended Jan. 31 after a 2% decline in the previous week. Refinancing activity improved due to a drop in mortgage rates, but new home applications fell due to rising home prices.

Total crude oil inventories rose by 8.9 million barrels in the week ended Jan. 31, with commercial oil inventories up 8.7 million barrels and stocks in the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve up 300,000 barrels. Gasoline inventories rose sharply while distillate inventories decreased.

The Q1 GDPnow estimate from the Atlanta Fed is for a 2.9% gain, revised down from a 3.9% gain reported on Monday. The next update is scheduled for Friday.

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1447 ET - Tariffs would likely complicate the Fed's job in responding to further stubbornness in inflation, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee says. Speaking at a conference in Detroit, Goolsbee says that depending on White House policy, the Fed will have to determine whether tariff-induced price increases are a one-time adjustment or reflect a deeper pattern before reacting. "If we see inflation rising or progress stalling in 2025, the Fed will be in the difficult position of trying to figure out if the inflation is coming from overheating or if it's coming from tariffs," Goolsbee says. "That distinction will be critical for deciding when or even if the Fed should act." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1445 ET - A third large weekly inventory draw leaves U.S. distillate stocks 12% below their five-year average as a colder-than-expected January drove extra demand for heating fuel. The inventory decline "is not too surprising given where refineries are operating, still below 85% of operable capacity, but that is something that creates near-term price risk for diesel," says Matt Muenster, chief economist at transport technology firm Breakthrough. Monday's pause in threatened tariffs on Canadian oil took some pressure off prices, and although the cost would likely be shared among Canadian oil producers, U.S. refiners and consumers, "if they had stayed, that ultimately was going to get passed to transportation pretty quickly," he adds. Also offsetting the price impact of lower diesel stocks is the seasonal build in crude oil inventories, Muenster says. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1431 ET - Front-month gold futures settle again at a new record, with that contract closing up 0.6% to $2,871.60 a troy ounce. It's the third consecutive session that gold climbed, and the sixth positive close in the past seven sessions. Amid confusion surrounding U.S. tariffs and retaliation, physical gold demand is strong, says Phil Flynn of Price Futures Group. "We're starting to see huge demand for physical gold and reports that the Comex exchange is having to secure more supplies to satisfy massive gold deliveries," says Flynn in a note. SPDR Gold shares are also higher in late trading, with shares up 0.8%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1332 ET - Stanley Black & Decker is making $150 million in targeted investments this year aimed at accelerating its supply chain overhaul to reduce the company's reliance on production in China, CFO Patrick Hallinan says on an analyst call. The tool maker anticipates that Donald Trump's targeted tariffs on China could cost the company $90 million to $100 million in 2025 if they remain in place, though Stanley believes it could get the impact down to $10 million to $20 million with higher prices and further changes to its supply chain, the CFO says. Stanley is waiting for greater clarity on tariffs before enacting any new measures to battle the cost inflation, the CFO says. "We expect the current situation to remain dynamic," he says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1323 ET - Brazil's soybean harvest is progressing slowly, the country's agricultural agency says. Amid the volatility surrounding tariffs, previously announced U.S. soybean sales may end up being cancelled in favor of cheaper Brazilian alternatives, says Brian Hoops of Midwest Market Solutions. Hoops says that unless the U.S. and China announce some sort of agreement for China to buy more U.S. grains, it's unlikely that China will alter its trend of favoring Brazilian exports over the U.S., a sentiment that extends into corn as well. "I would not be surprised to see some cancellations at some point this month as South American supplies become more plentiful," says Hoops. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1315 ET - Even with uncertainty around tariffs and trade negotiations dominating the thoughts of traders and analysts, they've also needed to focus on weather in South America. The forecast has turned more supportive for large crops in both Brazil and Argentina, with much of Argentina receiving rainfall this week. "The South American forecast is consistent in projecting the best rain in weeks across central Argentina in the next 48 hours," says Daniel Flynn of Price Futures Group in a note. "Regional drought persists in Argentina mainly in the north and across La Pampa, but rain this week will impact the highest concentrated corn/soy producing area of the county." Meanwhile, dryness in some areas of Brazil are allowing harvesting of soybeans there. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

Rymer also says Chipotle is confident it can offset the potential headwind through investments made in 2024 including through efficiencies identified in its supply chain. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1223 ET - Oil prices slide 2% despite U.S. President Trump reinstating maximum pressure on Iran, as rising U.S. crude inventories and fears of a trade war raise demand concerns. Brent crude is down 1.9% to $74.75 a barrel, while WTI falls 2.1% to $71.21 a barrel. The latest data from the Energy Information Administration show crude stockpiles rose by 8.7 million barrels last week, against expectations of a 1.3-million-barrel rise. Prices are also pressured by concerns that a trade spat between the U.S. and China could hurt global growth and raise inflationary pressures. "The oil market is now caught between increasing fears that an escalating trade war will damage global oil demand growth...and possible sudden disruption of Iranian oil exports," says SEB's chief commodities analyst Bjarne Schieldrop in a note. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1213 ET - Apple shares are under modest pressure amid reports that Chinese regulators are considering opening a formal investigation into App Store's fees and policies, even if revenue from the App Store in China is a manageable $5 billion, Wedbush analysts say in a research note. "So its less about revenue exposure for investors and more about building US/China tensions with US Big Tech in line for retaliatory shots across the bow," the analysts say, noting that China recently opened an antitrust probe into Alphabet's Google. "This is all a game of high stakes poker between Trump and China and Apple is clearly a chip on the table," the analysts add. Apple shares fall 1% to $230.39. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1159 ET - The threat of U.S. tariffs on imports from its northern neighbor brought out waves of calls on social media for Canadians to buy local products in place of American goods. Canada's largest private sector union wants to go further, and has written to the country's largest grocery chains urging them to buy Canadian. Unifor in a letter to the CEOs of Empire, Loblaw and Metro encouraged them to prioritize Canadian products in response to President Trump's threatened levies. It also wants grocers to make it easier for consumers to prioritize Canadian items on their shelves, supporting domestic industries and jobs. "Canadian workers and their families are ready to defend against the Trump tariff threat and we need Canadian grocers to do their part," says Unifor national President Lana Payne. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1149 ET - Finning International's answers to the threat of U.S. tariffs will be to reduce its cost profile. In an earnings call, executives said that its response to the near-term market environment of slower growth and higher uncertainty, "we are focused on managing working capital levels and evaluating opportunities to create further sustainable cost efficiency." In particular, the focus will be to improve the cost-structure in Canada. And the company already has good model to follow: "leveraging the structural changes and overhead reduction strategy demonstrated in our U.K. operations." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1139 ET - Griffon CEO Ronald Kramer says on a call with analysts that he's comfortable backing the company's 2025 outlook, calling the tariff situation extremely fluid. "Similar to tariffs in 2018, we expect to mitigate the effects of changing tariff policy through a combination of price, supply renegotiations and further diversifying our global supply chain," he says. The company has "significant amounts" of product coming from China in its lawn and garden and Hunter Fan businesses, but those are the only area where tariffs could have an effect, Kramer adds. Shares rise 10% after reporting better-than-expected earnings in its fiscal 1Q. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1447 ET - Tariffs would likely complicate the Fed's job in responding to further stubbornness in inflation, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee says. Speaking at a conference in Detroit, Goolsbee says that depending on White House policy, the Fed will have to determine whether tariff-induced price increases are a one-time adjustment or reflect a deeper pattern before reacting. "If we see inflation rising or progress stalling in 2025, the Fed will be in the difficult position of trying to figure out if the inflation is coming from overheating or if it's coming from tariffs," Goolsbee says. "That distinction will be critical for deciding when or even if the Fed should act." (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1333 ET - Global sustainable bond issuance is expected to remain steady in 2025, at about $1 trillion, according to S&P Global Ratings in a report. The agency says "with green bonds and supranational entities in the lead, the sustainable bond market seems to have returned to its roots." Corporate green bond issuance is now well established, S&P says. However, issuance fell in 2024 to about 11% of the global total, from 13% in 2023. More issuance in low-income and emerging economies is expected to address the climate finance gap, S&P says, but adds "we see a risk that macroeconomic and political factors could stifle further growth." (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1216 ET - Mexican household consumption rose 0.5% in November after two successive monthly declines, statistics institute Inegi reports. Fixed investment edged up 0.1% but was down 0.3% from a year earlier. "Headwinds for domestic demand persist, with consumption more resilient, but investment facing greater challenges," analysts at Banorte say in a note, referring to trade risks and a tight government budget. "Although tariffs against our country are on hold, we believe that the business climate will remain deteriorated until a definitive resolution is reached," and that probably won't happen until the USMCA review, they add. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1213 ET - Look for another month of strong employment growth on Friday, but don't be surprised if the labor market loses some momentum in the months ahead, Piper Sandler economists write. Their model projects that the economy added 209,000 jobs in January, more than the 169,000 consensus forecast in WSJ's poll. But Piper Sandler notes that small business hiring looked tepid in the January ADP data, "a concerning signal for overall employment," the economists say. Meanwhile, in the weekly UI benefits data, continuing unemployment claims have been elevated, which suggests more modest payroll numbers in the months ahead, Piper Sandler notes. ( matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1040 ET - The National Bank of Poland's more restrictive policy stance compared to other central banks has boosted the Polish zloty, ING analyst Frantisek Taborsky says. The NBP on Wednesday left its benchmark interest rate at 5.75%, as widely expected, having held rates steady throughout 2024. With the market pricing in more rate cuts for the European Central Bank, the zloty is supported by favorable rate differentials, Taborsky says. Another supportive factor is Poland's economic outperformance versus Central and Eastern European peers. "As a wild card for the zloty is the possible progress of a Ukraine-Russia deal, which would be positive for CEE especially Poland." EUR/PLN falls 0.2% to 4.2023, having earlier hit a 2018 low of 4.1931, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1038 ET - The U.S. Treasury refunding announcement "was mostly uneventful," Santander's Stephen Stanley writes. The U.S. Treasury says it is offering $125 billion in securities to refund around $106.2 billion of notes and bonds maturing this month, while raising around $18.8 billion in new cash. The Treasury warns of greater-than-normal issuance variability until the debt limit is suspended or increased. Stanley says the Treasury's current schedule "should be fine for most or all of calendar 2025." He says coupon auction sizes will likely need to increase. Treasury yields are falling, showing no reaction to the refunding announcement. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1032 ET - Tariff "front-running" likely contributed to December's increase in Canadian trade activity, which alongside the weaker Canadian dollar supported economic growth into end-2024, BMO Economics' Shelly Kaushik says. Canada swung to a C$708 million goods-trade surplus with the world in December, though the full-year shortfall deepened to C$7.2 billion. Kaushik says not to be surprised if early 2025 trade figures are subject to heightened volatility as everyone awaits more certainty on the U.S. tariff front. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbNMStewart)

1028 ET - U.S. growth came in surprisingly strong in 2024 versus start-of-year projections, and most economists expect the momentum to carry into 2025. But while the consumer economy and the services sector are thriving, industrial companies are complaining of a grimmer backdrop. "Recent economic activity is primarily being led by the strength in service-related sectors," while the physical economy has been weaker, Dow CFO Jeff Tate tells analysts on a recent earnings call. Dow executives several times on the call referred to a "prolonged economic downturn." Eastman Chemical CEO Mark Costa gave a similar take on the company's recent analyst call. "The macro economy is certainly challenging right now as we all know," he said. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1014 ET - Equinor cuts capital expenditure as expected, but shareholder distributions are disappointing, Jefferies analysts Giacomo Romeo and Kai Ye Loh write. Updated guidance reflects consensus expectations in terms of the capex cut, due to lower renewables spending. However, 2025 distributions are lower than expected and the outlook for 2026 distributions remains unclear, they say. The 2025 dividend and buyback of $9 billion is below consensus at $10 billion, while Equinor has scrapped its 2026 buyback guidance. Quarterly earnings beat consensus on net income but are relatively inline on the operating line and on cash flow from operations. Shares trade 3.5% lower at 268.20 Norwegian kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1002 ET - A new Redfin report reveals that nearly 70% of single, divorced, or separated individuals struggle with rent or mortgage payments, compared to over half of married people. Those living with a partner but unmarried face the highest struggle at over 75%. Single individuals often rely on one income, with 63% earning under $50,000 annually, versus 26% of married couples. Conversely, 29% of married people earn over $100,000, compared to just 7% of singles and 6% of divorced. Married couples benefit from tax advantages, enhancing financial stability. Despite these struggles, most pay on time, Redfin says. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0917 ET - A surge in Canadian exports in December and recent improvement in export-order survey indicators add to evidence the country's economy picked up momentum at the end of 2024, Capital Economics' Stephen Brown says. That puts some upside risk to the flash estimate GDP rebounded by only a modest 0.2% on-month in December after contracting 0.2% in November, he says. Brown adds the situation for exporters finally improved, at least until President Trump came within hours of imposing a 25% tariff. "That risk is likely to hang over the economy for some time." (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0910 ET - A surge in the U.S. trade deficit points to businesses getting in orders ahead of the imposition of tariffs on imports, says Thomas Ryan, an economist at Capital Economics. America's trade in goods and services with the rest of the world showed a sharply increased deficit in December to just shy of $100 billion, according to Commerce Department figures set out Wednesday. Imports rose and exports declined. "The strength of imports appears largely driven by businesses rushing orders ahead of potential tariffs, a trend unlikely to reverse any time soon," Ryan tells investors in a note.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: US natgas prices climb 3% to one-week high on colder forecasts, rising LNG flows
data.description:
Key points:
  • US gas output hit daily record high of 106.5 bcfd on January 31
  • Storage withdrawals may have hit monthly record high in Jan
  • US LNG export feedgas on track for record high in February

U.S. natural gas futures climbed about 3% to a one-week high on Wednesday on rising gas flows to liquefied natural gas export plants, and forecasts for colder weather and more heating demand next week than previously expected.

Front-month gas futures for March delivery on the New York Mercantile Exchange rose 10.7 cents, or 3.3%, to settle at $3.360 per million British thermal units (mmBtu), their highest close since Jan. 29.

Financial firm LSEG said average gas output in the Lower 48 U.S. states rose to 106.0 billion cubic feet per day so far in February, up from 102.7 bcfd in January when freezing oil and gas wells and pipes, known as freeze-offs, cut production, according to LSEG data. That compares with a monthly record of 104.6 bcfd in December 2023.

On a daily basis, output was on track to fall by around 1.3 bcfd over the past five days to a preliminary one-week low of 105.3 bcfd on Wednesday, down from a daily record high of 106.5 bcfd on January 31. The prior all-time high was 106.4 bcfd in January 2024.

After extreme cold last week boosted heating demand to a record high, analysts said energy firms may have pulled a record amount of gas out of storage this month. The current record monthly storage withdrawal is 994 billion cubic feet in January 2022, according to federal energy data.

Meteorologists projected weather in the Lower 48 states would switch from warmer than normal from February 5-8 to mostly colder than normal from February 9-14 and mostly near normal from Feb. 15-20.

With colder weather coming, LSEG forecasts average gas demand in the Lower 48 states, including exports, will rise from 123.1 bcfd this week to 134.1 bcfd next week. The forecast for this week was lower than LSEG's outlook on Tuesday, while its forecast for next week was higher.

The amount of gas flowing to the eight big U.S. LNG export plants rose to an average of 15.0 bcfd so far in February, up from 14.6 bcfd in January. That compares with a monthly record high of 14.7 bcfd in December 2023.

The U.S. became the world's biggest LNG supplier in 2023, ahead of recent leaders Australia and Qatar, as much higher global prices feed demand for more exports, due in part to supply disruptions and sanctions linked to Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine.

Gas was trading near a 15-month high of around $16 per mmBtu at the Dutch Title Transfer Facility (TTF) (TRNLTTFMc1) benchmark in Europe. In Asia, meanwhile, gas was trading around $14 at the Japan Korea Marker (JKM) (JKMc1) benchmark.

data.title: OSI Systems Executive Chairman Deepak Chopra Sells 40,000 Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameChopra Deepak
RelationshipEXECUTIVE CHAIRMAN
TypeSell
Amount$7,836,200
SEC FilingForm 4

Deepak Chopra, Executive Chairman of OSI Systems, sold 40,000 shares of common stock on February 3 and 4, 2025, for a total sale amount of $7,836,200. The sales were executed at weighted average prices of $193.55 and $198.26, respectively, across multiple trades. Following these transactions, Chopra directly owns 364,478 shares and indirectly owns 5,000 shares through The Deepika Chopra Trust.

SEC Filing: OSI SYSTEMS INC [ OSIS ] - Form 4 - Feb. 05, 2025
